title: Why Brand Equity Matters
coreIdea: Strong brand equity is not a vanity measure. PepsiCo uses BBE because stronger Demand Power and Perceived Value are linked to stronger growth outcomes and more resilient brand value.
brandManagerTakeaway: Brand Doctor helps you understand whether your brand is building the equity strength that can support future growth.
insightsLeadDetail: Demand Power and Perceived Value are the BBE outcome KPIs. Momentum on these outcomes is the enterprise ambition because statistically significant improvement is stronger evidence that brand equity is truly strengthening.
example: A brand can still be large and famous, but if Demand Power or Perceived Value momentum is slipping, Brand Doctor should surface that as a trajectory warning.
misreadWarning: Do not assume a strong current score means the brand is getting stronger. Current strength and Momentum are different.
dialogPrompts: Explain why brand equity matters in plain English.
Why can a strong brand still need attention?
What does Momentum add beyond current strength?

title: The BBE System in One Picture
coreIdea: BBE has outcome KPIs and input KPIs. Demand Power and Perceived Value are the outcomes; Salient, Meaningful, and Different explain the equity foundation underneath.
brandManagerTakeaway: The outcome KPIs tell you whether equity can support demand and perceived value. The input KPIs tell you what part of the equity system needs work.
insightsLeadDetail: Demand Power is typically read through relevance and mental availability; Perceived Value is typically read through relevance and distinctiveness. Use natural-language equivalents in narrative, while preserving construct names in method areas.
example: If Salient is strong but Different is weak, the brand may be easy to think of but not clear enough in why it should be chosen.
misreadWarning: Do not jump from one weak metric to a recommendation. Weak metrics are signals; the strategic read comes from the pattern.
dialogPrompts: Explain Salient, Meaningful, and Different.
How do input KPIs connect to Demand Power and Perceived Value?
What is the difference between a signal and a strategic read?

title: How to Read the Three Benchmarks
coreIdea: Every BBE metric should be read through three different lenses: how it indexes versus the category, whether it is Ahead of similar-size peers, and whether it has Momentum versus the prior period.
brandManagerTakeaway: Red does not always mean the same thing. A brand can be strong versus the category but still not Ahead of its peer ambition, or strong today but declining in Momentum.
insightsLeadDetail: Vs Category is a contextual index against category average. Ahead compares to top-third thresholds for similar size or life-stage brands. Momentum is statistically significant change versus the prior period.
example: Lay’s can be category-leading on Demand Power but still be flagged for declining Momentum. That is not a contradiction; it means current strength and trajectory are telling different parts of the story.
misreadWarning: Do not treat all red/amber/green signals as interchangeable. Always ask: red versus what? Category, peer ambition, or prior period?
dialogPrompts: What does Ahead mean?
How is Momentum different from Vs Category?
Give me an example of a benchmark conflict.

title: Why Momentum Is the Ambition
coreIdea: The ambition is to gain Momentum on Demand Power and Perceived Value, not merely to hold them. Holding can preserve current strength, but gaining Momentum tells us the brand is truly improving.
brandManagerTakeaway: A growth-ready brand is not only strong today; it is moving in the right direction for the role it needs to play.
insightsLeadDetail: Momentum is the equity trend signal that helps determine whether the brand is building, plateauing, or eroding. It should be interpreted with category context, role, and Growth Navigator conversion signals.
example: A strong but declining brand needs a different action path from a weaker brand that is gaining. One may need protection or refresh; the other may need acceleration.
misreadWarning: Do not assume holding is sufficient for active growth roles. Holding may be acceptable for some contexts, but it does not necessarily build future advantage.
dialogPrompts: Why is gaining Momentum the ambition?
How should I interpret holding?
What is the difference between strong but slipping and weak but gaining?

title: Source Context and Typology Boundaries
coreIdea: Some source decks include typology labels such as Star or Mainstream. Brand Doctor treats them as source context only; the product verdict should come from Momentum, Ahead/Behind, category context, M/D/S, Perceived Value, evidence gaps, and treatment paths to test.
brandManagerTakeaway: Use typology language only when someone is asking about the source context. Do not let a label replace the current brand read.
insightsLeadDetail: Source typology can explain how a source deck framed a brand in a specific market, category, period, and competitive set, but ordinary equity, CMO, momentum, and treatment answers should not volunteer those labels as the diagnosis.
example: If a source deck calls a brand a Star, Brand Doctor may explain what that source label meant when asked, but the headline read should still come from the current evidence pattern.
misreadWarning: Do not use typology labels as the product verdict or as permanent brand DNA.
dialogPrompts: When should typology language appear?
Why should Star not be the headline verdict?
How do source labels differ from the product read?

title: How BBE Connects to Growth Navigator
coreIdea: BBE diagnoses the equity system. Growth Navigator shows whether that equity is converting through proposition, reach, resonance, availability, visibility, and value delivery.
brandManagerTakeaway: If equity looks strong but growth is weak, do not automatically rewrite the brand. Check whether reach, availability, visibility, or value delivery is blocking conversion.
insightsLeadDetail: GN Vitals should be source-period aware. BBE and GN sources may not be perfectly aligned, so bridge language should avoid causal overclaiming unless the evidence supports it.
example: Tostitos and Cheetos can show different commercial signals even when both have equity watchouts. The strategic read must separate an equity issue from a conversion leak.
misreadWarning: Do not treat BBE and GN as duplicate dashboards. BBE explains equity strength; GN explains commercial vitals and conversion signals.
dialogPrompts: How does Growth Navigator help explain the brand read?
What is a commercial conversion leak?
When should we check availability before changing the brand?

title: Perceived Value Guardrail
coreIdea: Perceived Value is about whether brand equity broadly supports value perception. It is not a SKU price optimizer and should not be used for specific price-point decisions.
brandManagerTakeaway: Perceived Value can tell you whether the brand has equity permission for premium justification. For exact price, pack, or promo decisions, involve RGM and pricing science.
insightsLeadDetail: Perceived Value should be used as an equity signal, not as a substitute for conjoint, trade-off, Newton Miller Smith, MMM, or store/channel-level pricing analysis.
example: A brand with weak Perceived Value may need to strengthen distinctiveness and value cues before taking price, but the exact price move requires separate pricing analysis.
misreadWarning: Do not recommend SKU-level price changes from Perceived Value alone.
dialogPrompts: What can Perceived Value tell me?
What can Perceived Value not tell me?
When should I refer this to RGM?

title: How Brand Doctor Reads The Brand
coreIdea: Brand Doctor now works as one brand intelligence brain with many governed outputs. Start at the Brand Command Home, ask Jarvis or the stable Assistant, and open durable workspaces when the question needs proof, review gates, or a meeting-ready asset.
brandManagerTakeaway: You can start with the clear brand read, then use Jarvis, Report, Data, or Work depending on the decision moment.
insightsLeadDetail: Rules, evidence, treatment matching, asset composition, and review gates stay governed. The LLM explains and prepares approved work, but proof contracts and source boundaries remain visible.
example: If the read is Strong but Slipping, the Report explains the diagnosis, Jarvis can answer follow-ups, and the Work Shelf can hold a CMO Review asset, Evidence Read, or Treatment Read with proof and gaps visible.
misreadWarning: Generated workspaces are review drafts with export and circulation gates. They do not replace the cross-functional team’s decision.
dialogPrompts: Where should I go after the brand home?
What belongs in Report versus Work?
How do governed workspaces stay safe?

title: Connecting BBE to Better Marketing Action
coreIdea: BBE is the signal-reading spine. Connected systems and governed output workspaces help explain the pattern, reveal what evidence is missing, and shape smarter action paths to test.
brandManagerTakeaway: Start with the BBE read, then choose the next surface: Report for the full diagnosis, Jarvis for questions, Data for proof, or Work for a review-draft asset.
insightsLeadDetail: Growth Navigator, Mental Availability / CEPs, distinctive asset evidence, physical availability, machine availability, Evidence Readiness, and executive asset proof contracts are supporting lenses around the BBE read. Label evidence as measured, inferred, simulated, or missing and avoid creating a competing master score.
example: If Salient is weak, Mental Availability and CEP evidence can show which buying situations are underbuilt. If a leadership meeting is coming, the Work Shelf can hold a CMO Review or Evidence Read that keeps proof and caveats in flow.
misreadWarning: Do not treat connected systems as a replacement for BBE. They are evidence and action lenses that help marketers understand and act on the BBE read.
dialogPrompts: How do connected systems support BBE?
What is Evidence Readiness?
When should I open a work asset instead of the report?
